questions.question: Which 802.11 physical layer offers more capacity?
questions.explanation: 802.11a offers more capacity because it operates in the 5 GHz band and supports up to 54 Mbps using OFDM with 8 non-overlapping channels, compared to 802.11b (11 Mbps) and 802.11g (54 Mbps but with more interference in 2.4 GHz). However, note that 802.11a's 5 GHz band provides greater channel capacity with less congestion.
questions.takeaway: 802.11a = Higher capacity via 5 GHz band, OFDM, 54 Mbps, 8 non-overlapping channels

questions.question: What frequency band does 802.11g operate in?
questions.explanation: 802.11g operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical) band, providing backward compatibility with 802.11b devices. It supports speeds up to 54 Mbps using OFDM modulation.
questions.takeaway: 802.11g = 2.4 GHz band, up to 54 Mbps, backward compatible with 802.11b

questions.question: Spread Spectrum and CDMA are used in _______
questions.explanation: Third Generation (3G) mobile networks use Spread Spectrum and CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access) technologies. 1G used analog signals, 2G used TDMA/GSM, and 4G uses OFDMA/LTE technologies.
questions.takeaway: 3G = Spread Spectrum + CDMA; 2G = TDMA/GSM; 4G = OFDMA/LTE

questions.question: SSL contains two sub protocols:
options: Neither Record protocol nor Handshake protocol
Both Record protocol and Handshake protocol
questions.explanation: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) consists of two main sub-protocols: the Record Protocol, which handles data encryption, compression, and integrity checking; and the Handshake Protocol, which manages authentication and key exchange between client and server before data transfer begins.
questions.takeaway: SSL = Record Protocol (data security) + Handshake Protocol (authentication & key exchange)

questions.question: The Security layer in WAP is similar to which layer in the OSI model?
questions.explanation: The WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security) security layer in WAP (Wireless Application Protocol) is functionally similar to the Presentation Layer (Layer 6) of the OSI model, as it handles data encryption, decryption, and secure communication formatting.
questions.takeaway: WAP Security Layer (WTLS) ≈ OSI Presentation Layer — handles encryption and secure formatting

questions.question: Bluetooth works on
questions.explanation: Bluetooth operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical) band, specifically in the 2.400–2.4835 GHz range. It uses frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS) to avoid interference with other devices sharing the same band.
questions.takeaway: Bluetooth = 2.4 GHz ISM band + Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)

questions.question: An arbitrary collection of Bluetooth-enabled devices physically close to each other is called a:
questions.explanation: A Piconet is an ad hoc network formed by a collection of Bluetooth-enabled devices in close physical proximity. It consists of one master device and up to seven active slave devices. A Scatternet is formed when two or more Piconets are linked together.
questions.takeaway: Piconet = 1 master + up to 7 slaves (Bluetooth ad hoc); Scatternet = multiple linked Piconets

questions.question: The range of Bluetooth is:
questions.explanation: Standard Bluetooth (Class 2) has a typical range of approximately 30 feet (10 meters). Class 1 Bluetooth devices can reach up to 100 meters, while Class 3 is limited to about 1 meter. The most common consumer Bluetooth devices use Class 2 with a ~30-foot range.
questions.takeaway: Bluetooth Class 2 (standard) range = ~30 feet (10 meters)

questions.question: Attributes related to Wireless Security are (choose two):
questions.explanation: Wireless security is primarily built around the CIA triad. Confidentiality ensures data is accessible only to authorized users, and Integrity ensures data has not been tampered with during transmission. Availability and Utility are also important but Confidentiality and Integrity are the core wireless security attributes highlighted here.
questions.takeaway: Wireless Security core = Confidentiality + Integrity (part of the CIA triad)

questions.question: WAP applications are (choose two):
questions.explanation: WAP (Wireless Application Protocol) was designed for simple, low-bandwidth mobile applications. Classic WAP use cases include checking train timetables and looking up stock values — these involve lightweight data retrieval. Online banking and shopping typically require more complex secure transactions not suited for early WAP environments.
questions.takeaway: WAP typical apps = simple info retrieval tasks like train tables and stock lookups

questions.question: The WML declaration is known as:
questions.explanation: In WML (Wireless Markup Language), the XML declaration at the top of the document is known as the Document Prologue. It identifies the document as an XML/WML file and specifies encoding and DTD (Document Type Definition) information.
questions.takeaway: WML XML declaration = Document Prologue

questions.question: ________ element is used for emphasis in WML.
questions.explanation: In WML (Wireless Markup Language), the <em> element is used to apply emphasis to text, typically rendering it in italics. This is inherited from HTML/XHTML conventions and is the standard inline emphasis element.
questions.takeaway: <em> = emphasis/italics in WML (same as HTML)

questions.question: The mode attribute is related with which WML element?
questions.explanation:
In WML, the mode attribute is associated with the

(paragraph) element. It controls text wrapping behavior — the mode can be set to 'wrap' (text wraps automatically) or 'nowrap' (text does not wrap within the paragraph).
questions.takeaway:
WML mode attribute →

element; controls text wrapping (wrap/nowrap)

questions.question: The difference between HTML and WML is:
options: Tags are used in HTML but not in WML
A document in HTML is a page while in WML it is a card
questions.explanation: The primary structural difference is that in HTML, the basic unit of content is a 'page', while in WML (Wireless Markup Language), the basic unit is a 'card'. Multiple cards are grouped into a 'deck' (the WML document). Both HTML and WML use tag-based markup.
questions.takeaway: HTML = page-based; WML = card-based (multiple cards form a deck)

questions.question: Metadata is placed between which element in WML?
questions.explanation: In WML, metadata (such as document-level properties, cache control, and access control settings) is placed within the <head> element, just like in HTML. The <head> section contains elements like <meta>, <access>, and <template> that define document-level behavior.
questions.takeaway: WML metadata → inside <head> element (same convention as HTML)

questions.question: Which of the following is the task element in WML?
questions.explanation: In WML, <go> is a task element used to navigate to a URL or another card. Task elements define actions to be performed when a user interacts with the application. Other task elements include <prev>, <refresh>, and <noop>.
questions.takeaway: WML task elements = <go>, <prev>, <refresh>, <noop>; <go> navigates to a URL/card

questions.question: Preformatted text is specified by which tag in WML?
questions.explanation: The <pre> tag in WML (inherited from HTML) is used to define preformatted text. Text within <pre> tags is displayed in a fixed-width font and preserves whitespace, spaces, and line breaks exactly as written in the source.
questions.takeaway: <pre> = preformatted text in WML (preserves whitespace and line breaks)

questions.question: The image format used for WML images is:
questions.explanation: WML uses the .wbmp (Wireless Bitmap) format for images. WBMP is a monochrome (black and white) image format optimized for low-bandwidth, limited-display wireless devices. It was the mandatory image format for early WAP/WML browsers.
questions.takeaway: WML image format = .wbmp (Wireless Bitmap) — monochrome, low-bandwidth optimized

questions.question: WML variables are:
questions.explanation: WML (Wireless Markup Language) variables are case sensitive, meaning 'MyVar' and 'myvar' are treated as two different variables. This is an important distinction when writing WML scripts and referencing variables in WML documents.
questions.takeaway: WML variables = Case Sensitive ('MyVar' ≠ 'myvar')

questions.question: The align attribute is related with which WML element?
questions.explanation:
In WML, the align attribute is used with the

(paragraph) element to control text alignment. It can be set to 'left', 'right', or 'center' to align the text content within the paragraph on the wireless device display.
questions.takeaway:
WML align attribute →

element; values: left, right, center

questions.question: Which of the following pieces of information can be found in the IP header?
options: Both source address and destination address
Source address of the IP packet
Sequence number of the IP packet
Destination address for the IP packet
questions.explanation: The IP header contains both the source IP address and the destination IP address of the packet. The sequence number is a TCP-level concept found in the TCP header, not the IP header. IP operates at the Network Layer and is responsible for logical addressing and routing.
questions.takeaway: IP header = Source IP + Destination IP; Sequence numbers belong to TCP header

questions.question: Which of the following are application-level encryption protocols used for secure online banking?
questions.explanation: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) and SET (Secure Electronic Transaction) are application-level encryption protocols used for secure online transactions. SSL secures the communication channel, while SET was specifically designed for credit card transactions. VeriSign is a CA (Certificate Authority), SHA1 is a hashing algorithm, and PGP/PEM are email encryption standards.
questions.takeaway: Secure online banking protocols = SSL (channel security) + SET (transaction security)

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for ordered delivery of packets?
questions.explanation: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) of the OSI model is responsible for ordered, reliable, end-to-end delivery of packets. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) at this layer uses sequence numbers to ensure packets are reassembled in the correct order and retransmits lost packets.
questions.takeaway: Transport Layer (Layer 4) = Ordered, reliable delivery via TCP sequence numbers

questions.question: What port must be opened on a firewall to allow HTTPS (SSL-encrypted) traffic?
questions.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP over SSL/TLS) uses port 443 by default. Port 161 is used by SNMP, port 53 is used by DNS, and port 6000 is used by X11. Opening port 443 on a firewall allows SSL-encrypted web traffic to flow in both directions.
questions.takeaway: HTTPS = Port 443; HTTP = Port 80; DNS = Port 53; SNMP = Port 161

questions.question: A network point that provides entrance into another network is called a ___________
questions.explanation: A Gateway is a network device that serves as an entry/exit point between two different networks, often connecting networks that use different protocols. It operates at multiple OSI layers and can perform protocol translation. Routers route packets within the same protocol family, while switches operate within a single network.
questions.takeaway: Gateway = Entry/exit point between different networks; performs protocol translation

questions.question: What is the difference between a switch and a hub?
options: Switches operate at the transport layer while hubs operate at the physical layer
Switches operate at the data link layer while hubs operate at the transport layer
Switches operate at the physical layer while hubs operate at the data link layer
Switches operate at the data link layer while hubs operate at the physical layer
questions.explanation: Switches operate at Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) of the OSI model and use MAC addresses to forward frames to specific ports, creating separate collision domains. Hubs operate at Layer 1 (Physical Layer) and simply broadcast signals to all connected devices without any intelligence.
questions.takeaway: Switch = Layer 2 (MAC-based forwarding); Hub = Layer 1 (dumb broadcast to all ports)

questions.question: Which is a Layer 2 device in networking?
questions.explanation: A Switch operates at Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) of the OSI model, using MAC addresses to intelligently forward frames only to the relevant port. Hubs are Layer 1 devices, Routers are Layer 3 devices, and Bridges also operate at Layer 2 but switches have largely replaced them.
questions.takeaway: Layer 1 = Hub; Layer 2 = Switch, Bridge; Layer 3 = Router

questions.question: The port number for DNS is:
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) uses port 53 for both UDP (for standard queries) and TCP (for zone transfers and large responses). Port 43 is WHOIS, port 80 is HTTP, and port 110 is POP3 (email retrieval).
questions.takeaway: DNS = Port 53 (UDP for queries, TCP for zone transfers)

questions.question: The Internet is an example of a:
questions.explanation: The Internet is a packet-switched network. In packet switching, data is broken into packets that can take different routes to the destination and are reassembled at the receiver. Circuit switching (used in traditional telephony) establishes a dedicated path for the duration of the communication.
questions.takeaway: Internet = Packet-switched network; Traditional telephony = Circuit-switched

questions.question: Which of the following are true about firewalls? (Select all that apply.)
options: Can be either hardware or software
Can be configured to drop packets
questions.explanation: All listed statements about firewalls are true. Firewalls filter network traffic based on defined rule sets (ACLs), can exist as hardware appliances or software solutions, and can be configured to drop, reject, or accept packets based on criteria like IP address, port, and protocol.
questions.takeaway: Firewall = Traffic filter (hardware/software), rule-based, can drop/accept/reject packets

questions.question: Firewalls are designed to perform all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Traditional firewalls are NOT designed to protect against viruses. Virus protection is the role of antivirus/antimalware software. Firewalls limit security exposures by controlling network access, log Internet activity for auditing, and enforce security policies through rule sets, but they do not scan for or remove malware.
questions.takeaway: Firewall does NOT protect against viruses — that's antivirus software's job

questions.question: Kerberos is a:
options: Both single party and third party authentication service
questions.explanation: Kerberos is a third-party authentication service (network authentication protocol) developed at MIT. It uses a trusted third-party Key Distribution Center (KDC) to authenticate users and services without transmitting passwords over the network, using time-stamped tickets.
questions.takeaway: Kerberos = Third-party authentication using KDC (Key Distribution Center) and tickets

questions.question: You want to have a private, encrypted, and authorized communication between two sites. Which is the best choice?
questions.explanation: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) provides encrypted, authenticated, private communication tunnels between two sites over a public network like the Internet. Modems provide connectivity, firewalls filter traffic, and Bastion Hosts are hardened systems in DMZs — none of these provide end-to-end encryption between two sites like a VPN does.
questions.takeaway: VPN = Encrypted + Authenticated private tunnel over public networks between sites

questions.question: Which protocols should you use to secure a VPN connection? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: IPSec (Internet Protocol Security) is the primary protocol suite for securing VPN connections at the network layer, providing encryption, authentication, and data integrity. L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol) is often combined with IPSec (L2TP/IPSec) for VPN security. SSL/TLS are used in SSL VPNs. All listed protocols can be used, but IPSec is the most fundamental VPN security protocol.
questions.takeaway: VPN security protocols = IPSec (network layer), L2TP/IPSec, SSL/TLS VPN

questions.question: Which of the following firewall types will allow for port security?
questions.explanation: Packet filtering firewalls operate at the Network/Transport layer and can filter traffic based on source/destination IP addresses and port numbers, enabling port security. Stateful inspection tracks connection state. Application filtering operates at Layer 7. Packet filtering is the basic type that explicitly controls access by port number.
questions.takeaway: Packet filtering firewall = Controls access by IP address and port number (port security)

questions.question: Which of the following firewall policies is most restrictive?
questions.explanation: 'Deny all' (also called default-deny or implicit deny) is the most restrictive firewall policy. It blocks all traffic that is not explicitly permitted by a rule. 'Any any' or 'Permit all' policies allow all traffic, which is the least restrictive. Security best practice is to start with 'deny all' and only allow necessary traffic.
questions.takeaway: Most restrictive firewall policy = Deny All (default-deny); Security best practice

questions.question: Which of the following characteristics of an intranet are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: An intranet may be restricted to a community of users
An intranet can work with WANs
An intranet can be part of a LAN
An intranet is designed to be publicly available
questions.explanation: An intranet is a private, internal network that uses Internet technologies (TCP/IP, HTTP). It can be hosted within a LAN or span multiple sites via WAN. It is NOT publicly available — it is restricted to authorized users (employees or a specific community). Options A, C, and D are true; B is false.
questions.takeaway: Intranet = Private internal network (LAN/WAN capable), restricted to authorized users only

questions.question: What will a certificate server do?
options: Generates certificates for all certificate servers in the public network
Accepts digital signatures and verifies the same
questions.explanation: A Certificate Server (Certificate Authority) accepts and processes certificate requests from users or systems, verifies the requester's identity, and issues digital certificates. It maintains a Certificate Revocation List (CRL) and manages the certificate lifecycle. It does not simply verify digital signatures — that is the role of a relying party using the public key.
questions.takeaway: Certificate Server (CA) = Accepts, processes, and issues digital certificates

questions.question: The phrase __________ describes viruses, worms, Trojan horse attack applets, and attack scripts.
questions.explanation: Malware (malicious software) is the umbrella term that describes all harmful software including viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, spyware, adware, and attack scripts. Spam refers to unsolicited emails, phishing is a social engineering attack, and 'virus' is only one specific type of malware.
questions.takeaway: Malware = Umbrella term for all malicious software (viruses, worms, Trojans, spyware, etc.)

questions.question: The __________ of a threat measures its potential impact on a system.
questions.explanation: The 'degree of harm' of a threat measures its potential impact on a system if exploited. Vulnerabilities are weaknesses in a system, countermeasures are defensive actions taken to reduce risk, and susceptibility refers to how likely a system is to be affected. Impact/degree of harm quantifies the damage a successful attack could cause.
questions.takeaway: Degree of harm = Potential impact of a threat on a system if successfully exploited

questions.question: Nessus is an example of:
questions.explanation: Nessus, developed by Tenable, is one of the most widely used vulnerability scanners. It identifies security vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, missing patches, and compliance issues across networks, systems, and applications. While it can identify open ports, its primary function is comprehensive vulnerability assessment.
questions.takeaway: Nessus = Vulnerability scanner (by Tenable); identifies security flaws, misconfigs, missing patches

questions.question: Which vulnerability scanner identifies the operating system name and version?
questions.explanation: Nmap (Network Mapper) performs OS fingerprinting and can detect the operating system name and version of remote hosts using techniques like TCP/IP stack analysis. Nmap's -O flag enables OS detection. Amap performs application/service version detection, while Nessus focuses on vulnerability scanning.
questions.takeaway: Nmap = OS fingerprinting + port scanning; use -O flag for OS detection

questions.question: Ethereal and Snort are popular examples of:
questions.explanation: Ethereal (now known as Wireshark) is a network packet analyzer (sniffer) used to capture and analyze network traffic. Snort is an open-source network intrusion detection system (IDS) that also analyzes packets. Both tools capture and inspect network packets, classifying them as sniffer/packet analysis tools.
questions.takeaway: Ethereal (Wireshark) = Packet sniffer/analyzer; Snort = IDS/packet sniffer; both analyze network traffic

questions.question: What does OWASP stand for?
questions.explanation: OWASP stands for Open Web Application Security Project, a nonprofit organization that publishes widely trusted guidance on web application security including the Top 10 vulnerability list updated periodically.
questions.takeaway: OWASP = Open Web Application Security Project (nonprofit, web security standards body)

questions.question: Which vulnerability is ranked #1 in OWASP Top 10 – 2021?
questions.explanation: In OWASP 2021, Broken Access Control moved to #1. It occurs when users act outside their intended permissions — e.g., accessing other users' accounts or unauthorized admin functions.
questions.takeaway: OWASP 2021 #1 = Broken Access Control (was #5 in 2017)

questions.question: What was ranked #1 in OWASP 2017 but dropped to #3 in 2021?
questions.explanation: Injection was the #1 risk in OWASP 2017 but dropped to #3 in 2021, replaced by Broken Access Control at the top.
questions.takeaway: Injection: OWASP 2017 #1 → OWASP 2021 #3

questions.question: Which category is NEW in OWASP Top 10 – 2021 and focuses on design-level flaws?
questions.explanation: Insecure Design (A04:2021) is new in OWASP 2021 and focuses on risks from missing or ineffective security controls at the design/architecture level, not just implementation bugs.
questions.takeaway: A04:2021 Insecure Design = New OWASP 2021 entry; design-level security flaws

questions.question: Which OWASP 2021 category replaced 'Sensitive Data Exposure' from 2017?
questions.explanation: Cryptographic Failures (A02:2021) is the renamed and expanded version of Sensitive Data Exposure, more accurately describing the root cause — cryptographic failures leading to data exposure.
questions.takeaway: A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures = Renamed Sensitive Data Exposure (2017)

questions.question: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) was first added as a standalone category in which OWASP list?
questions.explanation: SSRF (A10:2021) appeared as a new standalone category in OWASP Top 10 – 2021. It occurs when a server fetches a remote resource using an attacker-controlled URL, potentially reaching internal services.
questions.takeaway: SSRF = New in OWASP 2021 (A10); server fetches attacker-controlled URLs → internal network access

questions.question: Which OWASP 2021 category covers Log4Shell-type vulnerabilities?
questions.explanation: Vulnerable and Outdated Components (A06:2021) covers use of components with known CVEs. Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) in Apache Log4j is a prime example — a critical RCE via a dependency.
questions.takeaway: Log4Shell → A06:2021 Vulnerable and Outdated Components

questions.question: Which OWASP 2021 category addresses insufficient logging and monitoring?
options: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures
questions.explanation: A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures covers inadequate logging, alerting, and response. Without proper monitoring, breaches go undetected for long periods.
questions.takeaway: A09:2021 = Security Logging & Monitoring Failures — undetected breaches persist longer

questions.question: What is SQL Injection?
options: A method to optimize SQL queries
An attack inserting malicious SQL into inputs to manipulate a database
questions.explanation: SQL Injection inserts malicious SQL statements into user-supplied input fields that are concatenated into database queries, potentially revealing, modifying, or deleting database data.
questions.takeaway: SQL Injection = Malicious SQL in user input → manipulate database queries

questions.question: Which SQL injection payload makes a WHERE clause always evaluate to true?
questions.explanation: ' OR '1'='1 appended to a WHERE clause makes the condition always true (1=1), causing the query to return all rows — the classic SQL injection test payload.
questions.takeaway: ' OR '1'='1 = Classic SQLi always-true payload; returns all rows

questions.question: Which type of SQL injection infers data through true/false application responses?
questions.explanation: Blind Boolean-based SQLi sends queries that produce different application responses for true vs false conditions. The attacker infers data one bit at a time by observing these behavioral differences.
questions.takeaway: Blind Boolean SQLi = True/false response differences reveal data bit by bit

questions.question: Which SLEEP() function is used in time-based blind SQL injection?
questions.explanation: SLEEP(n) in MySQL pauses the database for n seconds. In time-based blind SQLi, the attacker uses IF(condition, SLEEP(5), 0) to confirm injection — response delay indicates the condition is true.
questions.takeaway: Time-based Blind SQLi = SLEEP(n) causes delay → confirms injection via response time

questions.question: Which tool automates SQL injection detection and exploitation?
questions.explanation: SQLmap is an open-source tool that automates detection and exploitation of SQL injection vulnerabilities across MySQL, MSSQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, and other backends.
questions.takeaway: SQLmap = Automated SQL injection tool; supports all major databases

questions.question: What is the most effective defense against SQL injection?
questions.explanation: Parameterized queries separate SQL structure from user data. The database treats input as data only, never as executable SQL, making injection impossible regardless of input content.
questions.takeaway: SQLi defense #1 = Parameterized queries / Prepared statements

questions.question: Injection in stored procedures is possible when:
options: Stored procedures always prevent SQL injection
Dynamic SQL is built inside the procedure using user input concatenation
questions.explanation: Stored procedures are NOT inherently safe if they build dynamic SQL strings internally using EXEC() or sp_executesql with concatenated user input. The vulnerability is inside the stored procedure itself.
questions.takeaway: Stored procedures + dynamic SQL concatenation = still SQLi-vulnerable

questions.question: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
options: Injecting malicious scripts into web pages that execute in other users' browsers
questions.explanation: XSS injects malicious client-side scripts (JavaScript) into web pages. When victims view the page, the script runs in their browser, enabling cookie theft, session hijacking, or malware delivery.
questions.takeaway: XSS = Injecting malicious JS into pages → executes in victim's browser

questions.question: Which XSS type is permanently stored on the server and affects every visitor?
questions.explanation: Stored (Persistent) XSS saves the malicious script in the server's database or storage. Every subsequent visitor who loads the affected page executes the attacker's script automatically.
questions.takeaway: Stored XSS = Script in server storage; attacks every visitor automatically

questions.question: In Reflected XSS, the payload is:
options: Reflected off the server in the HTTP response to the victim who clicks a crafted link
questions.explanation: Reflected XSS embeds the payload in a URL. When the victim clicks the link, the server includes it in the response and the browser executes it. It requires the victim to click the crafted link.
questions.takeaway: Reflected XSS = Payload in URL, reflected in response; victim must click crafted link

questions.question: Which HTTP header mitigates XSS by controlling permitted script sources?
questions.explanation: CSP (Content-Security-Policy) specifies which sources are allowed to execute scripts. A strict CSP like script-src 'self' blocks all inline scripts and unauthorized external script sources.
questions.takeaway: CSP = HTTP header controlling script sources; primary XSS mitigation

questions.question: DOM-based XSS differs because:
options: The vulnerability exists in client-side JS — server never sees the payload
It only works in Internet Explorer
The payload is in the server database
questions.explanation: DOM-based XSS occurs entirely in the browser. Client-side JavaScript writes user-controlled data to the DOM (via innerHTML, document.write, eval). The server never processes the payload.
questions.takeaway: DOM-based XSS = Client-side JS vulnerability; server never sees payload

questions.question: What primary defense prevents XSS from executing injected code?
questions.explanation: Output encoding converts <, >, &, " to HTML entities (<, >, &, "). Injected scripts render as visible text rather than executing as code.
questions.takeaway: XSS defense = Output encoding; <script> becomes <script> — displayed not executed

questions.question: What is CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)?
options: Tricking an authenticated user's browser into making unauthorized requests to a trusted site
questions.explanation: CSRF exploits the fact that browsers auto-send cookies with requests. An attacker tricks an authenticated user's browser into sending a malicious request to a site where the user is logged in.
questions.takeaway: CSRF = Tricks authenticated browser into unauthorized state-changing request using user's session

questions.question: What is the most effective CSRF defense?
questions.explanation: Synchronizer CSRF tokens are unique, unpredictable values tied to the user's session, embedded in forms and validated server-side. An attacker cannot forge a valid request without knowing the token.
questions.takeaway: CSRF defense = Synchronizer token; server validates unique unpredictable token per request

questions.question: The SameSite cookie attribute protects against CSRF by:
options: Restricting cookies from being sent in cross-site requests
questions.explanation: SameSite=Strict prevents the browser from sending cookies in any cross-site request. SameSite=Lax restricts it to unsafe methods. Both prevent CSRF attacks that rely on auto-sent cookies.
questions.takeaway: SameSite=Strict/Lax cookie attribute = Blocks cross-site cookie submission → CSRF protection

questions.question: What is a buffer overflow attack?
options: Writing more data into a buffer than allocated, overwriting adjacent memory
questions.explanation: A buffer overflow writes past a buffer's boundary into adjacent memory. An attacker can overwrite the return address, function pointers, or other data to corrupt program behavior or execute arbitrary code.
questions.takeaway: Buffer overflow = Write past buffer boundary → overwrite adjacent memory → possible code execution

questions.question: A stack-based buffer overflow typically overwrites which memory value to redirect execution?
options: The return address on the call stack
questions.explanation: Stack-based overflows overflow a buffer on the call stack to overwrite the saved return address. When the function returns, execution jumps to the attacker-controlled address (often shellcode).
questions.takeaway: Stack BOF = Overwrite return address on stack → redirect to shellcode

questions.question: Which protection randomizes stack/heap/library base addresses to hinder exploitation?
questions.explanation: ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) randomizes memory region base addresses each run, making it difficult for attackers to predict where to jump after a buffer overflow.
questions.takeaway: ASLR = Randomizes memory layout → attackers can't predict jump addresses

questions.question: What does DEP/NX do to prevent buffer overflow exploitation?
options: Places canary values before return addresses
Marks stack/heap as non-executable so injected shellcode cannot run
questions.explanation: DEP (Windows) / NX (Linux/CPU) marks memory regions like the stack and heap as non-executable. Even if shellcode is injected via a buffer overflow, the CPU refuses to execute data from non-executable regions.
questions.takeaway: DEP/NX = Non-executable stack/heap; shellcode injected but cannot execute

questions.question: What is a NOP sled in buffer overflow exploitation?
options: A sequence of NOP instructions before shellcode to increase the chance of landing in the shellcode regardless of the exact jump address
questions.explanation: A NOP sled is a sequence of 0x90 (NOP) instructions placed before shellcode. If execution lands anywhere in the NOP sled, it slides down to the shellcode, improving exploit reliability when the exact address is uncertain.
questions.takeaway: NOP sled = 0x90 instructions before shellcode; execution slides to shellcode even with imprecise jump

questions.question: What is an Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)?
options: Application uses user-controlled input (ID/filename) to access objects without authorization checks
questions.explanation: IDOR occurs when an app exposes internal object references (account IDs, file paths) and doesn't verify the requesting user's authorization. E.g., changing account_id=100 to 101 accesses another user's data.
questions.takeaway: IDOR = User-controlled ID/reference → access unauthorized objects without permission check

questions.question: The principle of least privilege means:
options: Users receive only the minimum permissions required for their role
All access denied by default with no exceptions
questions.explanation: Least privilege grants users, processes, and systems only the access rights necessary for their legitimate tasks. Excess permissions increase risk if an account or process is compromised.
questions.takeaway: Least privilege = Minimum permissions only; limits damage from compromised accounts

questions.question: Which access control model grants permissions based on job roles?
questions.explanation: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns permissions to predefined roles (Admin, User, Manager), then assigns users to roles. It simplifies management in large organizations and enforces least privilege.
questions.takeaway: RBAC = Role-based permissions; Admin/User/Manager roles carry specific access rights

questions.question: Path traversal attacks use which sequence to navigate above the web root?
questions.explanation: Path traversal uses ../ sequences (or encoded variants like %2e%2e%2f) to navigate up directories beyond the web root, potentially accessing /etc/passwd or other sensitive server files.
questions.takeaway: Path traversal = ../ sequences to escape web root → access sensitive files like /etc/passwd

questions.question: What is a Denial of Service (DoS) attack?
options: An attack overwhelming a system to deny legitimate users access
questions.explanation: A DoS attack floods a target with traffic or requests to exhaust resources (bandwidth, CPU, memory connections), making it unavailable. DDoS uses many attacking machines simultaneously for greater impact.
questions.takeaway: DoS = Overwhelm system → deny legitimate access; DDoS = Distributed DoS using botnets

questions.question: A SYN Flood attack exploits which protocol mechanism?
questions.explanation: SYN Flood sends many SYN packets without completing the TCP handshake (no final ACK). The server allocates state for each half-open connection until the table is full, blocking new legitimate connections.
questions.takeaway: SYN Flood = TCP half-open connections flood; server state table exhausted → service denied

questions.question: A Smurf attack amplifies traffic using:
options: ICMP echo requests to broadcast address with spoofed victim IP
questions.explanation: A Smurf attack sends ICMP echo requests to a network broadcast address with the victim's spoofed source IP. All hosts on the network reply to the victim, amplifying the traffic volume.
questions.takeaway: Smurf = ICMP broadcast + spoofed victim IP; all hosts reply to victim → traffic amplification

questions.question: Which DDoS attack type targets the application layer (Layer 7)?
questions.explanation: HTTP Flood and Slowloris are Layer 7 attacks. Slowloris keeps many HTTP connections open by sending partial requests, exhausting the web server's connection pool without high bandwidth.
questions.takeaway: Layer 7 DDoS = HTTP Flood, Slowloris; exhausts web server connections without high bandwidth

questions.question: What is a Botnet?
options: A collection of compromised computers controlled by an attacker to launch coordinated attacks
questions.explanation: A botnet is a network of malware-infected computers (bots) remotely controlled by an attacker (botmaster via C2 server). Botnets power DDoS attacks, spam campaigns, credential stuffing, and more.
questions.takeaway: Botnet = Compromised machines (bots) controlled via C2 server; used for DDoS, spam, attacks

questions.question: What is OWASP API Security Top 10 risk #1?
questions.explanation: BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization), also known as IDOR at the API level, is the #1 OWASP API risk. APIs expose object IDs but fail to verify whether the requesting user is authorized to access that specific object.
questions.takeaway: OWASP API #1 = BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) = IDOR for APIs

questions.question: What does REST stand for in RESTful APIs?
questions.explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and stateless communication for web APIs, identifying resources by URIs.
questions.takeaway: REST = Representational State Transfer; stateless HTTP-based API architecture

questions.question: What is Excessive Data Exposure in API security?
options: API returns all object fields relying on clients to filter, exposing sensitive data in raw responses
questions.explanation: Excessive Data Exposure sends all object fields in the API response, relying on the client to filter display. An attacker intercepting raw API responses sees fields not shown in the UI (tokens, PII, internal IDs).
questions.takeaway: Excessive Data Exposure = API returns all fields; attacker intercepts response to see hidden sensitive data

questions.question: What is the purpose of API rate limiting?
options: Limit the number of requests per client per time window to prevent abuse, DoS, and brute force
questions.explanation: Rate limiting controls the request frequency from a single client. It prevents DoS, brute-force password attacks, credential stuffing, and web scraping by rejecting requests that exceed the allowed threshold.
questions.takeaway: API Rate limiting = Max requests per time window; prevents DoS, brute force, scraping

questions.question: Which standard is most commonly used to secure REST APIs?
questions.explanation: OAuth 2.0 is the industry standard for API authorization. JWT (JSON Web Tokens) serve as the access token format. Together they provide stateless, scalable, and secure API authentication and authorization.
questions.takeaway: REST API security standard = OAuth 2.0 + JWT; stateless, scalable authorization

questions.question: What is SAST (Static Application Security Testing)?
options: Analyzing source code or binaries without execution to find vulnerabilities
questions.explanation: SAST analyzes source code, bytecode, or binaries for vulnerabilities without running the application. It integrates early in the SDLC (shift-left) and catches issues like SQLi, XSS, and buffer overflows in the code itself.
questions.takeaway: SAST = Static code analysis without execution; white-box; finds bugs early in SDLC

questions.question: What is DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing)?
options: Testing a running application by sending attacks and observing responses
questions.explanation: DAST tests a running application from the outside (black-box). It sends malicious inputs, analyzes responses, and finds runtime vulnerabilities like XSS, injection, and authentication flaws invisible in static code.
questions.takeaway: DAST = Tests running application from outside; black-box; finds runtime vulnerabilities

questions.question: Which popular open-source DAST tool is maintained by OWASP?
questions.explanation: OWASP ZAP is a free, open-source DAST tool with an intercepting proxy, active/passive scanner, fuzzer, and API scanner. It is widely used in DevSecOps pipelines for automated web application scanning.
questions.takeaway: OWASP ZAP = Free open-source DAST tool; intercepting proxy + scanner; CI/CD integration

questions.question: SonarQube is primarily used for:
options: Static code analysis and code quality assessment (SAST)
questions.explanation: SonarQube is a SAST and code quality platform that analyzes source code for bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code smells across many languages including Java, Python, JavaScript, and C#.
questions.takeaway: SonarQube = SAST + code quality; static analysis for security bugs and code smells

questions.question: Burp Suite is primarily used for:
options: Web application penetration testing with an intercepting proxy
questions.explanation: Burp Suite (PortSwigger) is the leading web application penetration testing tool. Its intercepting proxy captures and modifies HTTP/HTTPS traffic. Features include Scanner, Intruder, Repeater, Decoder, and Comparer.
questions.takeaway: Burp Suite = #1 web app pentesting tool; intercepting proxy + scanner + intruder + repeater

questions.question: 'Shift-left' security in SDLC means:
options: Shifting security responsibility to end users
Integrating security practices and testing earlier in the development lifecycle
Moving security to the production phase
questions.explanation: Shift-left means bringing security earlier in the SDLC — threat modeling during design, SAST during coding, security testing in CI/CD — rather than waiting for post-deployment pentests. Earlier fixes are far cheaper.
questions.takeaway: Shift-left = Security earlier in SDLC; findings in dev cost 10x less to fix than in production

questions.question: What is DevSecOps?
options: Integration of security practices throughout the DevOps pipeline as shared responsibility
A team of security-only DevOps engineers
questions.explanation: DevSecOps makes security everyone's responsibility — integrated into CI/CD pipelines with automated SAST, DAST, container scanning, and IaC security checks, rather than a separate gate at the end.
questions.takeaway: DevSecOps = Security integrated into DevOps CI/CD; everyone owns security throughout

questions.question: What is container image scanning in DevSecOps?
options: Detecting known CVEs in Docker images before deployment
questions.explanation: Container image scanning (Trivy, Clair, Snyk) analyzes Docker/OCI images for known CVEs in OS packages and application libraries before deployment, preventing vulnerable containers from reaching production.
questions.takeaway: Container image scanning = Find CVEs in Docker images pre-deployment; tools: Trivy, Clair, Snyk

questions.question: What is a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)?
options: A machine-readable inventory of all software components and dependencies in a product
questions.explanation: An SBOM is a formal inventory of all software components and their dependencies. Critical for supply chain security — when a vulnerability is found in a component (like Log4j), organizations can instantly identify all affected products.
questions.takeaway: SBOM = Inventory of all components/dependencies; critical for supply chain security (Log4j lessons)

questions.question: What is the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud security?
options: Security split between cloud provider (of cloud) and customer (in cloud)
A third-party auditor manages all security
questions.explanation: The Shared Responsibility Model: cloud providers secure 'of the cloud' (physical infrastructure, hypervisor, hardware), while customers secure 'in the cloud' (OS hardening, data, IAM policies, application security).
questions.takeaway: Cloud: Provider secures OF cloud (hardware/infra); Customer secures IN cloud (data/apps/IAM)

questions.question: What does IAM stand for in cloud security?
questions.explanation: IAM (Identity and Access Management) controls who (identity) can do what (action) on which cloud resources (effect). Misconfigured IAM — overly permissive policies, public buckets — is a leading cause of cloud breaches.
questions.takeaway: IAM = Identity and Access Management; controls cloud resource access via users/roles/policies

questions.question: Which cloud security risk involves publicly accessible storage buckets?
questions.explanation: Insecure Cloud Storage (publicly accessible S3 buckets, Azure Blob, GCS) has caused numerous major data breaches. Default-deny access configurations and regular audits are essential mitigations.
questions.takeaway: Insecure Cloud Storage = Public S3/Blob buckets → major data breach; audit access regularly

questions.question: What is cloud misconfiguration?
options: Incorrectly configured cloud services exposing resources to unauthorized access
A vendor bug in cloud infrastructure
questions.explanation: Cloud misconfiguration — open security groups, overly permissive IAM, disabled logging, public storage, default credentials — is the #1 cause of cloud security incidents, far exceeding platform vulnerabilities.
questions.takeaway: Cloud Misconfiguration = #1 cloud risk; open buckets, excess IAM, default creds, disabled logging

questions.question: What is an AWS Security Group?
options: An IAM group for security roles
A team of AWS security engineers
A virtual stateful firewall for EC2 instances controlling inbound/outbound traffic by port/protocol/IP
questions.explanation: AWS Security Groups are stateful virtual firewalls attached to EC2 instances. Rules specify allowed inbound/outbound traffic by port, protocol, and source/destination. Return traffic for allowed connections is automatically permitted.
questions.takeaway: AWS Security Group = Stateful EC2 virtual firewall; controls traffic by port/protocol/IP

questions.question: What is the OWASP Top 10 Cloud Security risk related to excessive permissions?
questions.explanation: Overprivileged IAM identities — roles and policies granting more permissions than needed — are a critical cloud risk. If compromised, an overprivileged identity can access, modify, or delete vast amounts of cloud resources.
questions.takeaway: Overprivileged IAM = Critical cloud risk; apply least privilege to all roles and policies

questions.question: What does IAST (Interactive Application Security Testing) combine?
options: Elements of both SAST and DAST by instrumenting the application during runtime testing
questions.explanation: IAST instruments the application with sensors/agents that monitor it from inside during runtime testing. It combines SAST's code-level insight with DAST's runtime testing, producing fewer false positives than either alone.
questions.takeaway: IAST = Instrumented runtime testing; combines SAST code insight + DAST runtime testing; fewer false positives

questions.question: What is Threat Risk Modelling?
options: A structured process to identify, quantify, and prioritize potential security threats against a system during design
questions.explanation: Threat Risk Modelling identifies potential threats against a system, assesses their likelihood and impact, and prioritizes security controls. STRIDE and DREAD are common frameworks used during the design phase.
questions.takeaway: Threat Risk Modelling = Identify+quantify+prioritize threats at design time; use STRIDE/DREAD

questions.question: What is DREAD in threat modelling?
options: A risk rating model: Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, Discoverability
questions.explanation: DREAD rates threats on five factors: Damage potential, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, and Discoverability. Each factor scores 1-10, and the average indicates overall threat severity.
questions.takeaway: DREAD = Damage + Reproducibility + Exploitability + Affected users + Discoverability; threat scoring

questions.question: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve a resource without modifying it?
questions.explanation: GET retrieves resources without side effects (idempotent and safe). POST creates, PUT updates (full replacement), PATCH updates (partial), DELETE removes. Sensitive data should never be sent via GET as it appears in URLs and logs.
questions.takeaway: HTTP: GET=Read, POST=Create, PUT=Full Update, PATCH=Partial Update, DELETE=Remove

questions.question: What security risk arises from verbose error messages in web applications?
options: Information disclosure — error messages revealing stack traces, DB type, file paths, or code structure
questions.explanation: Verbose error messages expose internal implementation details (database type, file paths, stack traces, framework versions) that attackers use to refine attacks. Production systems must use generic error messages.
questions.takeaway: Verbose errors = Information disclosure; hide error details in production; log internally only

questions.question: What is directory listing (directory traversal vulnerability at web server level)?
options: Web server returning a list of files in a directory when no index file exists, exposing sensitive files
questions.explanation: Directory listing occurs when a web server displays all files in a directory when no index.html exists. This can expose backup files, configuration files, source code, and other sensitive content. It should be disabled.
questions.takeaway: Directory listing = Web server exposes file list when no index exists; disable in web server config

questions.question: What is HTTP response splitting?
options: Injecting CRLF sequences into HTTP headers to split one response into two, enabling XSS or cache poisoning
A DoS via large HTTP responses
questions.explanation: HTTP Response Splitting injects CRLF (\r\n) characters into HTTP response headers to create a fake second response. Attackers can inject XSS payloads or poison web caches by controlling the 'second' response content.
questions.takeaway: HTTP Response Splitting = CRLF injection in headers → split response → XSS or cache poisoning

questions.question: What are the five phases of ethical hacking in order?
options: Threat Model, Vuln Assess, Exploit, Post-exploit, Remediate
Reconnaissance, Scanning, Gaining Access, Maintaining Access, Covering Tracks
questions.explanation: The five ethical hacking phases are: 1) Reconnaissance, 2) Scanning, 3) Gaining Access (exploitation), 4) Maintaining Access (backdoors/persistence), 5) Covering Tracks (removing evidence of intrusion).
questions.takeaway: Ethical Hacking: Recon → Scanning → Gaining Access → Maintaining Access → Covering Tracks

questions.question: What is footprinting in ethical hacking?
options: Collecting public information about a target before active testing
questions.explanation: Footprinting (reconnaissance) gathers information about a target using public sources — websites, WHOIS, DNS records, social media, job postings — without directly interacting with target systems.
questions.takeaway: Footprinting = Passive OSINT about target; WHOIS, DNS, website, social media — no direct interaction

questions.question: Which footprinting type uses OSINT without directly interacting with target systems?
questions.explanation: Passive footprinting gathers target information using publicly available sources (OSINT) without touching the target's systems, leaving no trace in target logs. Active footprinting involves direct target interaction (scanning, grabbing banners).
questions.takeaway: Passive footprinting = OSINT only, no target interaction, no logs; Active = direct probing

questions.question: What does WHOIS lookup reveal about a domain?
options: Domain registrant info, organization, email, nameservers, and registration/expiry dates
questions.explanation: WHOIS queries return domain registration data: registrant name, organization, contact email, nameservers, and registration/expiry dates. This information aids reconnaissance during ethical hacking engagements.
questions.takeaway: WHOIS = Domain registration details (registrant, email, nameservers) — key footprinting data

questions.question: What does a misconfigured DNS zone transfer (AXFR) reveal?
options: All DNS records of a domain — hostnames, IPs, subdomains — mapping the network
questions.explanation: DNS AXFR zone transfer copies all DNS records from the server. If allowed from any host (misconfigured), an attacker gets a complete map of all subdomains, internal hosts, and IP addresses.
questions.takeaway: DNS zone transfer = Full DNS record dump; reveals all hosts/IPs; restrict to authorized servers only

questions.question: Google Dorks use which technique in footprinting?
options: Advanced search operators to find sensitive information indexed by Google
questions.explanation: Google Dorks use operators like site:, filetype:, inurl:, intitle: to find accidentally indexed sensitive content — admin pages, configuration files, backup files, login portals, exposed credentials.
questions.takeaway: Google Dorks = Advanced Google operators to find exposed sensitive files/pages (OSINT)

questions.question: What is Shodan used for in reconnaissance?
options: Searching internet-connected devices and their open ports/services/banners
questions.explanation: Shodan continuously scans the internet and indexes data about internet-connected devices — servers, routers, IoT, ICS/SCADA — including open ports, software versions, and banners. A powerful OSINT reconnaissance tool.
questions.takeaway: Shodan = Internet device search engine; find exposed devices, open ports, software versions (OSINT)

questions.question: Recon-ng is used for:
options: Open-source intelligence (OSINT) gathering and reconnaissance automation
questions.explanation: Recon-ng is a modular web reconnaissance framework written in Python. It automates OSINT gathering from sources like WHOIS, DNS, Shodan, HaveIBeenPwned, and social media, similar to Metasploit's module approach.
questions.takeaway: Recon-ng = Modular OSINT framework for automated reconnaissance (DNS, WHOIS, social media)

questions.question: What is Traceroute used for in footprinting?
options: Mapping the network path (hops) packets take to a destination, revealing intermediate routers and network topology
questions.explanation: Traceroute sends packets with incrementing TTL values to discover each hop (router) between source and destination. The resulting path reveals network topology, ISPs, and potentially internal network structure.
questions.takeaway: Traceroute = Maps network path hop by hop; reveals routers and network topology between source and target

questions.question: What is port scanning?
options: Probing a host's TCP/UDP ports to discover which are open and what services are listening
Scanning physical network ports on a switch
questions.explanation: Port scanning probes TCP/UDP ports to determine which are open (service listening), closed (no service), or filtered (firewall). Open ports identify accessible services and potential attack entry points.
questions.takeaway: Port scanning = Identify open TCP/UDP ports and their services; Nmap is the primary tool

questions.question: What is Nmap's default scan type when run with root/admin privileges?
questions.explanation: Nmap's default scan with root privileges is the SYN scan. It sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK for open ports, then sends RST instead of completing the handshake — creating no full TCP connection and leaving fewer log entries.
questions.takeaway: Nmap default = SYN scan (stealth); SYN → SYN-ACK → RST; faster and stealthier than full connect

questions.question: A TCP XMAS scan sets which flags?
questions.explanation: XMAS scan sends packets with FIN, URG, and PSH flags set simultaneously (all 'lit up'). Per RFC 793, open ports ignore the packet; closed ports reply with RST. It doesn't work on Windows systems.
questions.takeaway: XMAS scan = FIN+URG+PSH flags; open=no reply, closed=RST; ineffective on Windows

questions.question: What does a TCP NULL scan send?
options: Packets with no TCP flags set at all
questions.explanation: A NULL scan sends TCP packets with no flags set. Open ports don't respond (per RFC); closed ports send RST. Like XMAS/FIN scans, it can evade simple SYN-blocking firewalls but fails against Windows hosts.
questions.takeaway: NULL scan = No TCP flags; open=silence, closed=RST; evades basic SYN-only firewalls

questions.question: An IDLE scan (zombie scan) uses which technique to achieve anonymity?
options: Using a third-party idle host's IP ID sequence to infer target port states without directly connecting to the target
questions.explanation: An IDLE scan exploits a zombie host's predictable IP ID counter. The attacker spoofs SYN packets from the zombie to the target, then monitors the zombie's IP ID to infer if target ports are open — the attacker's IP never appears in target logs.
questions.takeaway: IDLE/Zombie scan = Uses third-party host IP ID counter to scan target anonymously; attacker invisible

questions.question: What is banner grabbing used for?
options: Collecting service software name and version from open ports to identify vulnerable software
questions.explanation: Banner grabbing retrieves the identification banner sent by a network service on connection, often revealing software name, version, and OS. Attackers use this to identify outdated/vulnerable software versions.
questions.takeaway: Banner grabbing = Collect service/version info from open ports; find outdated vulnerable software

questions.question: Nmap's -sV flag is used for:
questions.explanation: Nmap's -sV flag enables service version detection. Nmap sends probe packets to open ports and analyzes responses to determine the exact software and version running (e.g., Apache 2.4.51, OpenSSH 8.2).
questions.takeaway: Nmap -sV = Service version detection; identifies exact software versions on open ports

questions.question: Nmap's -O flag performs:
options: OS fingerprinting using TCP/IP stack analysis
questions.explanation: Nmap's -O flag performs OS fingerprinting by analyzing TCP/IP stack characteristics (TTL, window size, TCP options) in responses to determine the target's operating system and version.
questions.takeaway: Nmap -O = OS fingerprinting; analyzes TCP/IP stack to determine target OS and version

questions.question: What is enumeration in ethical hacking?
options: Actively extracting usernames, network shares, services, and resources from target systems
questions.explanation: Enumeration actively queries target systems to extract detailed information — usernames, groups, shared resources, services, and OS details. Common techniques: NetBIOS, SNMP, LDAP, SMTP, and SMB enumeration.
questions.takeaway: Enumeration = Active extraction of users/shares/services from target; more invasive than scanning

questions.question: NetBIOS enumeration targets which ports?
questions.explanation: NetBIOS uses ports 137 (Name Service), 138 (Datagram), 139 (Session). SMB uses port 445. Together they expose Windows network shares, hostnames, domain info, and usernames during enumeration.
questions.takeaway: NetBIOS enumeration → ports 137-139 + SMB 445; extracts Windows usernames and shares

questions.question: SNMP enumeration with default community strings reveals:
options: Network device info, running processes, interfaces, routing tables via default 'public' community string
questions.explanation: SNMP with the default 'public' community string (read) or 'private' (write) can expose system description, running processes, network interfaces, ARP cache, routing tables, and user accounts on misconfigured devices.
questions.takeaway: SNMP enum = Default 'public'/'private' strings → leaks device config, processes, network details

questions.question: What is a brute force attack on passwords?
options: Systematically trying all possible character combinations until the correct password is found
questions.explanation: Brute force tries every possible password combination. It is guaranteed to succeed eventually but is slow for long, complex passwords. Time increases exponentially with password length and character set size.
questions.takeaway: Brute force = Try all combinations; guaranteed success but slow; defeated by long complex passwords

questions.question: What is a dictionary attack?
options: Using a wordlist of common passwords to attempt authentication — faster than brute force
questions.explanation: Dictionary attacks try passwords from a precompiled wordlist of common passwords, phrases, and variations. Far faster than brute force, they are highly effective against weak or common passwords.
questions.takeaway: Dictionary attack = Wordlist of common passwords; fast and effective against weak passwords

questions.question: What is credential stuffing?
options: Using username/password pairs from one data breach to attack other services, exploiting password reuse
questions.explanation: Credential stuffing uses leaked credentials from one breach to automatically try logging into other services. It exploits password reuse — many users use the same credentials on multiple sites.
questions.takeaway: Credential stuffing = Reuse breached creds from Site A on Site B; exploits password reuse

questions.question: Rainbow Table attacks are defeated by:
options: Adding a unique random salt per password before hashing
questions.explanation: Salting adds a unique random value to each password before hashing, making precomputed rainbow tables useless. Even identical passwords produce different hashes when salted with different values.
questions.takeaway: Salt defeats rainbow tables; unique per-password salt makes precomputed lookups impossible

questions.question: What is a pass-the-hash (PtH) attack?
options: Using a stolen NTLM hash directly to authenticate without needing the plaintext password
questions.explanation: Pass-the-Hash uses a stolen NTLM hash to authenticate to Windows systems without cracking it. Windows NTLM protocol accepts the hash directly. PtH enables lateral movement across a Windows network.
questions.takeaway: PtH = Use stolen NTLM hash for auth without cracking; enables lateral movement in Windows networks

questions.question: Which tool is purpose-built for GPU-accelerated password hash cracking?
questions.explanation: Hashcat is the world's fastest GPU-accelerated password cracker. It supports hundreds of hash types (MD5, NTLM, bcrypt, SHA-256, WPA2) and modes including dictionary, brute force, and rule-based attacks.
questions.takeaway: Hashcat = GPU-accelerated password cracker; supports 300+ hash types including NTLM and WPA2

questions.question: John the Ripper is used for:
options: Password hash cracking supporting multiple hash formats
questions.explanation: John the Ripper (JtR) is a popular open-source password cracker. It auto-detects hash types, supports dictionary and brute force modes, and includes rule-based mangling. Commonly used for Linux shadow file and Windows NTLM cracking.
questions.takeaway: John the Ripper = Open-source password cracker; auto-detects hash type; dictionary + brute force

questions.question: Which Kali Linux tool is used for online brute-force attacks against services like SSH and HTTP?
questions.explanation: Hydra (THC-Hydra) is a fast parallelized online password brute-force tool supporting SSH, FTP, HTTP, RDP, SMB, and dozens of other protocols. It is a standard tool for credential attack testing.
questions.takeaway: Hydra = Online brute-force tool; attacks SSH, FTP, HTTP, RDP, SMB; parallelized for speed

questions.question: Active online password attacks involve:
options: Capturing hash files and cracking offline
Directly attempting login with passwords against a live service
questions.explanation: Active online attacks directly interact with the target service (SSH, web login, FTP) to try passwords. They are slower, noisier (generate logs), and risk account lockouts. Passive/offline attacks crack captured hashes without touching the service.
questions.takeaway: Active online = Direct login attempts against live service; noisy, risks lockout; Offline = crack captured hashes

questions.question: What is LDAP enumeration used for?
options: Extracting user accounts, groups, organizational units, and attributes from Active Directory via LDAP queries
questions.explanation: LDAP enumeration queries Active Directory or other LDAP directories to extract user accounts, group memberships, organizational units, GPOs, and other directory attributes. Useful for mapping Windows domain environments.
questions.takeaway: LDAP enum = Extract AD users/groups/OUs via LDAP queries; maps Windows domain structure

questions.question: What is SMB Relay (NTLM relay) attack?
options: Intercepting NTLM authentication and relaying it in real time to another server to gain access without cracking the hash
questions.explanation: SMB/NTLM Relay intercepts NTLM challenge-response authentication and relays it to another server in real time. The attacker acts as MITM between victim and target server, gaining access without ever needing the plaintext password or cracking the hash.
questions.takeaway: SMB/NTLM Relay = Intercept NTLM auth → relay to another server → gain access without hash cracking

questions.question: What is a Trojan horse in cybersecurity?
options: Malware disguised as legitimate software that secretly performs malicious actions
questions.explanation: A Trojan disguises itself as a legitimate, useful program. Unlike viruses and worms it does not self-replicate. When run, it performs hidden malicious functions (backdoor, data theft, spying) alongside or instead of the promised legitimate function.
questions.takeaway: Trojan = Malware disguised as legit software; does NOT self-replicate; hides malicious payload

questions.question: What is a RAT (Remote Access Trojan)?
options: A Trojan giving attacker full remote control over the victim system
questions.explanation: A RAT gives an attacker complete remote control — file system access, screen capture, keylogging, webcam control, command execution. Common RATs: njRAT, DarkComet, Poison Ivy, AsyncRAT.
questions.takeaway: RAT = Remote Access Trojan; full attacker control: files, screen, keylog, webcam, commands

questions.question: What is a reverse shell (reverse-connecting Trojan)?
options: Connection initiated FROM the victim TO the attacker, bypassing inbound firewall rules
A shell that encrypts all connections
questions.explanation: A reverse shell has the victim machine connect outbound to the attacker's listener. Since firewalls typically block inbound connections but allow outbound, reverse shells bypass standard inbound firewall rules.
questions.takeaway: Reverse shell = Victim connects OUT to attacker; bypasses inbound firewall blocking

questions.question: What is a bind shell?
options: A shell that binds encryption keys
A listening port on the victim awaiting attacker's inbound connection
questions.explanation: A bind shell opens a listening port on the victim. The attacker connects to this port. Unlike reverse shells, bind shells require inbound access to the victim, which firewalls typically block. Reverse shells are generally preferred.
questions.takeaway: Bind shell = Victim listens, attacker connects in; often blocked by firewalls; reverse shell preferred

questions.question: What is Netcat called in the ethical hacking community?
options: The Swiss Army Knife of networking
questions.explanation: Netcat (nc) is called the Swiss Army Knife of networking due to its versatility: it creates TCP/UDP connections, acts as a client or server, transfers files, creates bind/reverse shells, and performs banner grabbing.
questions.takeaway: Netcat = Swiss Army Knife of networking; shells, file transfer, port scan, banner grab

questions.question: What is covert channel communication?
options: Using unintended system resources (ICMP, DNS, timing) to secretly transmit data bypassing security controls
questions.explanation: A covert channel uses resources not designed for communication — ICMP payloads, DNS query data, CPU timing — to secretly exfiltrate data. This bypasses security controls looking only at legitimate communication channels.
questions.takeaway: Covert channel = Exfiltrate data via unintended resources (DNS, ICMP, timing); bypasses controls

questions.question: What is Trojan wrapping (binding)?
options: Combining a Trojan with a legitimate program into one file so the victim unknowingly executes both
questions.explanation: A Trojan wrapper/binder joins the Trojan executable with a legitimate program. The victim runs the legitimate-looking file; both programs execute, with the Trojan running silently in the background installing a backdoor.
questions.takeaway: Trojan wrapper = Trojan bundled with legit software; victim runs legit file, Trojan installs silently

questions.question: Indications of a Trojan attack include:
options: Unexpected CPU usage, unknown processes, disabled security tools, unexpected network connections
questions.explanation: Trojan infection indicators include: unknown background processes, unexpected CPU/memory spikes, disabled antivirus, unusual network connections to unknown IPs, missing/modified files, unexpected program installations.
questions.takeaway: Trojan IOCs: unknown processes, disabled AV, unusual network connections, unexpected CPU spikes

questions.question: System File Verification (SFV) detects Trojan modifications by:
options: Comparing current system file hashes against a known-good baseline to detect unauthorized modifications
questions.explanation: SFV uses cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-256) of system files to create a known-good baseline. Trojans that modify system files change the hash, revealing tampering when compared against the baseline.
questions.takeaway: SFV = Hash comparison of system files vs baseline; detects Trojan file modifications

questions.question: What is the key difference between a virus and a worm?
options: Viruses spread via email only; worms via USB
Viruses need a host file + human action to spread; worms self-replicate and spread autonomously
questions.explanation: A virus attaches to a host file and needs human action (opening the file) to activate and spread. A worm self-replicates and spreads across networks autonomously — no host file or user action required.
questions.takeaway: Virus = Host file + human activation; Worm = Self-replicating, spreads automatically across networks

questions.question: A polymorphic virus evades detection by:
options: Hiding in the MBR (Master Boot Record)
Mutating its code signature with each infection while maintaining the same malicious functionality
questions.explanation: A polymorphic virus encrypts its body and changes the decryption routine with each infection, producing different file signatures. Signature-based AV cannot match a consistent pattern since the signature changes each time.
questions.takeaway: Polymorphic virus = Changes signature each infection; defeats signature-based AV

questions.question: What is a metamorphic virus?
options: A virus that rewrites its ENTIRE code body with each replication, making pattern-matching nearly impossible
questions.explanation: A metamorphic virus rewrites its entire code using code transposition, substitution, and garbage insertion with each replication. Unlike polymorphic (encrypted body, same core), metamorphic viruses are fundamentally different code each time.
questions.takeaway: Metamorphic = Entire code rewritten each replication; hardest to detect; beyond polymorphic

questions.question: What is ransomware?
options: Malware encrypting victim data and demanding payment (cryptocurrency) for the decryption key
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts a victim's files or locks the system, then demands ransom (usually Bitcoin/Monero) for the decryption key. Major families: WannaCry, REvil, LockBit, Conti, ALPHV/BlackCat.
questions.takeaway: Ransomware = Encrypts files → demands crypto ransom; WannaCry, LockBit, REvil are major families

questions.question: What is a keylogger?
options: Software or hardware that records all keystrokes to capture passwords, PINs, and sensitive input
questions.explanation: Keyloggers record every keystroke typed by the user, capturing passwords, credit card numbers, messages, and other sensitive data. Software keyloggers run as malware; hardware keyloggers are physical devices between keyboard and computer.
questions.takeaway: Keylogger = Records all keystrokes; captures passwords and sensitive input; software or hardware

questions.question: Antivirus evasion techniques used by malware include:
options: Polymorphism, metamorphism, packing/obfuscation, fileless techniques, and living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBins)
questions.explanation: Modern malware evades AV using: polymorphic/metamorphic code (changing signatures), packers/crypters (obfuscating code), fileless execution (running in memory, no file on disk), and LOLBins (abusing legitimate system tools like PowerShell).
questions.takeaway: AV evasion: polymorphism, packing/crypters, fileless execution, LOLBins (PowerShell, WMI)

questions.question: Fileless malware evades detection because:
options: It uses very small file sizes
It runs entirely in memory without writing to disk, leaving no persistent file for AV to scan
questions.explanation: Fileless malware executes entirely in memory (RAM) using legitimate tools like PowerShell, WMI, or Regsvr32. Since no malicious file is written to disk, file-based AV scanners find nothing. Memory forensics is required to detect it.
questions.takeaway: Fileless malware = Runs in memory only, no disk file; evades file-based AV; use memory forensics

questions.question: Which protocols are most susceptible to sniffing (transmit data in cleartext)?
questions.explanation: Protocols transmitting data in cleartext are susceptible to sniffing: HTTP (web), FTP (file transfer), Telnet (remote login), SMTP/POP3 (email). Their encrypted counterparts (HTTPS, SFTP/FTPS, SSH) are resistant.
questions.takeaway: Cleartext protocols = HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP, POP3 — all sniffable; use HTTPS, SSH, SFTP instead

questions.question: Active sniffing attacks in switched networks use which technique?
options: Simply plugging into a switch port
ARP poisoning or MAC flooding to redirect traffic to the attacker's NIC
questions.explanation: In switched networks, each port only receives its own traffic. Active sniffing uses ARP poisoning (redirect traffic via MITM) or MAC flooding (cause switch to broadcast all frames like a hub) to capture others' traffic.
questions.takeaway: Active sniffing in switched networks = ARP poison or MAC flood to redirect traffic to attacker

questions.question: ARP Poisoning works by:
options: Sending fake ARP replies associating the attacker's MAC with a legitimate IP, redirecting traffic for MITM
Flooding the network with ARP broadcasts
questions.explanation: ARP Poisoning sends gratuitous ARP replies mapping the attacker's MAC to a victim's IP (e.g., default gateway). Victims update their ARP cache and send traffic to the attacker's MAC, enabling MITM interception and forwarding.
questions.takeaway: ARP Poisoning = Fake ARP: attacker MAC ↔ legitimate IP → MITM traffic interception

questions.question: MAC Flooding causes a switch to act like a hub because:
options: Flooding with fake MACs exhausts the CAM table; switch enters fail-open mode broadcasting all frames to all ports
questions.explanation: MAC Flooding overwhelms the switch's CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table with thousands of spoofed MAC addresses. When full, the switch fails open and broadcasts all frames to every port, enabling passive sniffing.
questions.takeaway: MAC Flooding = Exhaust CAM table → switch broadcasts all frames (hub behavior) → passive sniff

questions.question: Ettercap is primarily used for:
options: ARP poisoning and MITM attacks in LAN environments
questions.explanation: Ettercap is a comprehensive open-source tool for LAN-based MITM attacks. It performs ARP poisoning, sniffs traffic, and supports plugins for credential capture, SSL stripping, and DNS spoofing.
questions.takeaway: Ettercap = ARP poisoning + MITM tool; sniffs credentials, supports SSL stripping, DNS spoofing

questions.question: DNS Spoofing (DNS Cache Poisoning) involves:
options: Injecting malicious DNS records into a resolver's cache to redirect users to attacker-controlled IPs
questions.explanation: DNS Cache Poisoning injects forged DNS responses into a recursive resolver's cache. When victims query the poisoned resolver, they receive the attacker's IP and are redirected to a malicious server without noticing.
questions.takeaway: DNS Cache Poisoning = Inject fake DNS records → victims redirected to attacker's server

questions.question: What is session hijacking?
options: Taking over an authenticated user's session by stealing or predicting the session token
questions.explanation: Session hijacking steals or forges a valid session token (cookie/session ID) to impersonate an authenticated user. Once the attacker has the token, they can access the user's account without credentials.
questions.takeaway: Session hijacking = Steal/forge session token → take over authenticated user's session

questions.question: The HttpOnly cookie flag prevents session hijacking by:
options: Preventing JavaScript from accessing the cookie via document.cookie, blocking XSS-based cookie theft
questions.explanation: HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie value (document.cookie returns nothing). This blocks XSS-based session cookie theft even if XSS is present on the page.
questions.takeaway: HttpOnly flag = JS cannot read cookie → blocks XSS-based session hijacking

questions.question: The Secure cookie flag ensures:
options: The cookie is only sent over HTTPS connections, preventing MITM theft on unencrypted connections
questions.explanation: The Secure attribute ensures the browser only sends the cookie over HTTPS. If a user accidentally visits HTTP, the browser does not send the cookie, preventing MITM theft of session tokens.
questions.takeaway: Secure flag = Cookie only over HTTPS; prevents MITM theft via HTTP interception

questions.question: Session fixation attack works by:
options: Fixing a broken session management system
Attacker sets a known session ID before the victim logs in; if not regenerated after login, attacker reuses it
